name: Tour of the Marina Alta — Circular through Montgó
name_original: Tour por la Marina Alta — Circular a través del Montgó
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Dénia traverse a diverse landscape, from coastal plains along the Mediterranean Sea to challenging mountain ranges inland. The region features the imposing Montgó Natural Park and the Sierra de Bèrnia, offering significant elevation gains and panoramic views. Cyclists can find routes winding through pine forests, past ancient fortresses, and through rural areas with orange and almond orchards. This varied terrain provides options for different road cycling preferences. Best road cycling routes around Dénia The most popular road cycling route is Tour around Les Valls - Circular from Dénia, a 60.6 miles (97.5 km) trail that takes 4 hours 44 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the valleys and rural landscapes surrounding Dénia. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Tour of the Marina Alta — Circular through Montgó, a difficult 43.1 miles (69.4 km) path. This route circles the Montgó massif, offering coastal and inland views. Local road cyclists also love the Coll de Rates – Sa Creueta Pass (780 m) loop from la Xara, a 48.1 miles (77.3 km) trail leading through the challenging Coll de Rates and Sa Creueta Pass, often completed in about 3 hours 57 minutes. Road cycling around Dénia is defined by coastal roads, mountain climbs, and rural greenways, with options for different ability levels. The routes in Dénia are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 500 reviews. More than 29,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Dénia's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Dénia?
A: Dénia offers a wide variety of road cycling routes, with over 135 different tours available. These routes cater to all levels, including 50 easy, 50 moderate, and 35 difficult options, ensuring there's something for every cyclist.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Dénia?
A: Road cycling in Dénia features a diverse landscape. You'll find everything from flat coastal roads offering stunning sea views, such as those along Les Rotes, to challenging mountain climbs in areas like the Montgó Natural Park and Sierra de Bèrnia. Many routes also wind through rural settings, passing orange and almond orchards.

Q: Are there challenging routes for experienced road cyclists in Dénia?
A: Absolutely. Dénia is known for its demanding climbs, particularly in the Sierra de Bèrnia, which features ascents with average gradients of 12.1% and sections exceeding 20%. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e890593714/tour-around-les-valls-circular-from-denia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour around Les Valls - Circular from Dénia&lt;/a&gt; (97.5 km with 1,371 m elevation gain) and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31373308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll de Rates – Sa Creueta Pass (780 m) loop from la Xara&lt;/a&gt; (77.3 km with 1,288 m elevation gain) offer significant challenges.

Q: Are there any easier or family-friendly road cycling routes in Dénia?
A: Yes, for a more relaxed experience, the 'Orange Route' (also known as the Dénia Greenway) is a flat and easy 6.5 km stretch along a former railway line, perfect for families. This route passes through picturesque orchards and can be extended towards the Pego-Oliva Marsh Natural Park. While primarily a greenway, it offers a gentle introduction to cycling in the region.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or landmarks I can see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer breathtaking views. You can cycle past the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/416894" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torre del Gerro&lt;/a&gt;, explore the natural sea cave &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/68080" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cova Tallada&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy panoramic vistas from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/709871" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vall d'Ebo Pass&lt;/a&gt;. The Montgó Natural Park also provides stunning coastal and inland views, especially from the ascent to Cabo de Sant Antoni.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Dénia?
A: Dénia's pleasant and warm climate makes it suitable for cycling year-round. However, for optimal comfort, spring and autumn are ideal. During summer, it's best to plan your rides for early morning or late afternoon to avoid the midday heat.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in Dénia?
A: Yes, many of Dénia's road cycling routes are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e892078673/tour-of-the-marina-alta-circular-through-montgo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour of the Marina Alta — Circular through Montgó&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19949042" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pego–Sagra Road – Vall d'Ebo Pass loop from la Xara&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are the options for parking when accessing road cycling routes in Dénia?
A: Dénia is cycling-friendly, and many routes, especially those in mountain or rural areas, utilize secondary roads with low traffic. While specific parking areas for each route aren't always designated, you can often find parking in the towns and villages that serve as starting points for these routes, such as Dénia itself or la Xara.

Q: Is public transport available for cyclists in Dénia?
A: While Dénia has public transport, specific information on bike allowances for all local services can vary. It's advisable to check with local bus or train operators directly if you plan to use public transport to reach your cycling starting point with your bike.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many routes pass through or near picturesque villages and towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel. The cycling-friendly culture in Dénia means you'll often find establishments catering to cyclists, especially along popular routes and in areas like Pedreguer.

Q: Do I need any special permits to cycle in natural parks like Montgó?
A: Generally, you do not need special permits for road cycling on established roads within natural parks like Montgó. However, it's always good practice to respect park regulations, stay on marked routes, and be mindful of wildlife and other park users. For specific details on off-road or mountain biking within the park, it's best to consult local park authorities.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Dénia?
A: The road cycling experience in Dénia is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 500 reviews. More than 29,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Dénia's varied terrain, often praising the diverse landscapes, challenging climbs, and scenic coastal roads.

Q: Are there any particularly long road cycling routes for endurance riders?
A: For endurance riders, Dénia offers several long-distance routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e890593714/tour-around-les-valls-circular-from-denia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour around Les Valls - Circular from Dénia&lt;/a&gt; covers 97.5 km, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19949042" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pego–Sagra Road – Vall d'Ebo Pass loop from la Xara&lt;/a&gt; is also a substantial 97.1 km, both offering significant elevation gains for a challenging ride.
tips:
Gata de Gorgos owes its name to the river (bed) the Gorgos that runs through the village. This river flows into the Mediterranean Sea at Jávea. The village is located at approximately 75m above sea level and has over 6000 inhabitants who mainly speak Spanish and Valencian. 

From the 16th to the 18th century, agriculture was the most important source of income for the village. The most important things that were grown during this time were: wheat, (wine) grapes, cane, olives, barley, almonds and, for example, grapes for raisins. Until the middle of the 20th century, agriculture was the most important financial sector for Gata de Gorgos. Due to the deterioration of the quality of the soil, but especially due to the demand for (second) homes on the coast, the former agricultural land has now been built on in many places by, among other things, urbanisation and tourism has become an important source of income. 

Gata de Gorgos has a very charming centre that is definitely worth a visit. Those looking for products made of reed will find what they are looking for here. The place is known for its handmade articles (artesanías) made of reed (hats, baskets and the like). The shops that sell these products have earned Gata de Gorgos its nickname ‘Bazaar of the Costa Blanca’.

Perhaps one of the most popular and renowned coves on the Costa Blanca. It is undoubtedly a beauty of a place and deserves a visit at any time of the year, just to clarify that in summer it is usually very crowded and it will be difficult to find a parking space.

A top location worth a visit. It offers fantastic views of the coast and the cliffs along the coastline. The road there is easy to navigate. The cape separates the bays of Alicante and Valencia and is surrounded by hidden coves and small islands. A striking landmark is the white lighthouse from 1928, which stands 120 meters above the sea and shines its light up to 23 nautical miles away. A visit is worthwhile not only for the impressive landscape, but also for the picturesque viewpoints that offer unforgettable panoramic views of the deep blue Mediterranean.

Another of the panoramic points with the best views of Jávea and the Marina Alta Coast. I recommend getting there on a clear day with good visibility, so you can enjoy the stunning views and unique landscape!

The road connects the cities of Denia and Jávea. It is a very beautiful climb with views of the sea and the mountains. The highest peak on the road is in the La Plana area. Here, there is a car park from where trails lead up to the top of the mountain.

The pavement of the road is in perfect condition. There is hardly any shoulder on the road and you have to be careful with the traffic, especially in the busiest months of the year.
